title: Grilled Corn and Seafood Recipes for a BBQ
keyword: grilled corn and seafood recipes for bbq
meta_description: Discover delicious grilled corn and seafood recipes for bbq season. From spicy shrimp boats to buttery lobster tails, elevate your cookout today!
html:
There is a specific kind of magic that happens when sweet, smoky corn meets briny, fresh seafood right over the open flame. If you are tired of the same old burgers and dogs, it is time to shake things up. We have curated a collection of the best grilled corn and seafood recipes for bbq nights that will impress your guests without keeping you tethered to the grill for hours.

This combination is a summer staple for a reason. The natural sugars in corn caramelize beautifully on the grates, providing a crunchy, sweet contrast to the tender, savory flavors of shrimp, crab, and lobster. Plus, pulling these meals together is often easier than you think. Let’s dive into how you can transform your patio into a seaside seafood shack.

The Perfect Pairing: Why Corn and Seafood Work

Before we get to the recipes, let’s talk about why this duo works so well. Corn acts as a blank canvas that absorbs the smokiness of the grill while retaining its internal sweetness. This sweetness acts as a perfect foil for salty seafood and spicy seasonings like Old Bay, chili powder, or paprika. Whether you are serving them side-by-side or combining them in a single dish, the textures complement each other perfectly—the pop of the corn kernel against the flake of a fish fillet or the snap of a shrimp.

Essential Tools for a Mess-Free Feast

Let’s be honest: eating corn on the cob can be a messy affair, especially when you are mixing it with melted butter and seafood seasoning. You want your guests to enjoy the food, not worry about butter running down their arms or the cob spinning out of their grip.

This is where having the right gadgets makes a difference. I highly recommend picking up the Girla Interlocking Corn on the Cob Holders Set - 5 Pairs (10 Pcs). These aren't your average flimsy skewers. They feature bright silicone handles that offer a non-slip grip, which is crucial when your hands are wet from the ocean or covered in marinade. Because they interlock, storage is a breeze—you won't have to dig through a junk drawer to find a matching pair. Plus, they are dishwasher-safe, so cleanup after a big seafood boil is one less thing to worry about.

Recipe 1: Foil-Packet Seafood and Corn Boils

This is arguably the easiest way to serve a crowd. It captures the low-country boil vibe without the massive stockpot.

  1. Prep the Corn: Cut ears of corn into 2-inch wheels (this makes them easier to eat and cook faster).
  2. Assemble: Tear off large sheets of heavy-duty foil. Place a quarter-pound of andouille sausage, half a dozen shrimp, a handful of clams, and a few pieces of corn on each sheet.
  3. Season: Drizzle generously with olive oil, minced garlic, lemon slices, and a heavy sprinkle of Old Bay seasoning.
  4. Cook: Fold the foil into tight packets, sealing the edges. Grill over medium-high heat for about 10–12 minutes, or until the clams open and the shrimp turn pink.
  5. Serve: Be careful opening the packets—steam will be hot. Pour the contents directly onto a platter.

Recipe 2: Grilled Lobster Tails with Street Corn

>

If you want to elevate your grilled corn and seafood recipes for bbq night to a luxury level, lobster tails are the way to go. The sweetness of the lobster matches the charred corn perfectly.

Start by butterflying your lobster tails. Use kitchen shears to cut down the center of the top shell, stopping at the tail fan, and gently pry the shell open to lift the meat. Brush the meat with a mixture of melted butter, garlic, paprika, and parsley.

For the corn, shuck the ears and brush them with a lime-infused butter. Grill the lobster tails meat-side down for about 5-6 minutes until they are opaque and lightly charred. Turn the corn frequently to get an even roast on all sides. Serve the lobster with a wedge of lemon and the corn on the cob secured with those Girla Interlocking Corn on the Cob Holders to make handling the hot cobs safe and elegant.

Recipe 3: Spicy Shrimp and Elote Skewers

This recipe deconstructs the Mexican street corn classic (Elote) and puts it on a stick alongside juicy shrimp.

Thread marinated jumbo shrimp onto metal skewers, alternating with cherry tomatoes. Grill these quickly—just 2-3 minutes per side. Meanwhile, grill your whole ears of corn until they have a nice char.

Once off the grill, brush the corn with a layer of mayonnaise (trust me on this), then roll it in cotija cheese and chili powder. Slice the corn off the cob or serve it whole. If serving whole, inserting the corn holders before applying the mayo and cheese keeps your fingers clean. Serve the spicy shrimp alongside the corn for a kick of heat that balances the creamy, tangy corn.

Tips for Grilling Corn Like a Pro

Getting the corn right is the foundation of these meals. Here is how to ensure it comes out perfect every time:

  • Soak the Husks: If you are grilling corn in the husk, soak the ears in cold water for 15 minutes beforehand. This prevents the husks from burning instantly and steams the kernels inside.
  • Direct vs. Indirect Heat: For husked corn, direct heat gives you that desired char. For un-husked corn, indirect heat is better to steam the kernels slowly.
  • Don't Overcook: Corn only needs about 10-15 minutes on the grill. Overcooking makes the kernels starchy and tough rather than sweet and juicy.

FAQ

How do I know when grilled corn is done?

The kernels should be tender and plump. If you are grilling husked corn, look for a golden-brown char on the tips of the kernels. You can also pierce a kernel with a paring knife; it should release a milky liquid easily.

Can I use frozen corn for these recipes?

While fresh corn is best for that crunch, you can use frozen corn kernels in foil packets or mixed into seafood salads. However, for corn on the cob, always use fresh ears to get the right texture and grill marks.

What other seafood pairs well with grilled corn?

Scallops are fantastic grilled alongside corn because they caramelize quickly. Mahi-mahi and halibut are also sturdy, meaty fish that hold up well on the grill and pair nicely with the sweetness of the corn.
